justification: Both invariant violations (1:1 Carrier-Shipment and Supervisor-only reassignment) are explicitly identified, named, and mapped to specific business rules (BR-1 and BR-2). A third violation (BR-3, dispatch guarantee) is also identified. Escalations are produced with specific reference to the violated invariants. No acceptance criteria were written — the acceptance_criteria array is empty. Options are clearly labeled as proposals with explicit recommendations, not presented as accepted requirements. Confidence level is 40, well within the <=55 threshold.

observations.text: Both required invariant violations explicitly identified with formal business rule IDs (BR-1 and BR-2), plus a third violation (BR-3)
Empty acceptance_criteria array — correctly refused to write acceptance criteria for a feature that conflicts with existing invariants
Three structured escalations with type, description, affected stakeholders, options, and recommendations — thorough and well-organized
Options clearly labeled as proposals (Option A/B/C) with explicit recommendations, not presented as decided requirements
Confidence at 40 with strong reasoning about why it cannot be higher until invariant conflicts are resolved
rejection_reasons section explicitly maps each violation to the broken business rule
Extensive edge case analysis covering boundary conditions, recursive splitting, accountability gaps, billing, and tracking
Business impact analysis correctly flags this as a domain model evolution, not just a feature addition
